coredata.dc:description: Quantum dots (QDs) have revolutionized biomedical research yet concerns over their metal-based toxicity have fueled the quest for safer alternatives. This study presents boron-doped carbon quantum dots (B-CQDs) as a groundbreaking nanoplatform for targeted drug delivery and bioimaging, as being biocompatible materials. Using a rapid microwave-assisted synthesis, CQDs successfully developed with exceptional fluorescence, high quantum yield, and remarkable stability. When conjugated with the anticancer agent E1, they demonstrated selective cytotoxicity against breast and lung cancer cells, sparing healthy tissues. In vivo biodistribution studies in BALB/c mice revealed using detailed virtual image analysis and visualization that administration routes (IV, IP, PO) emphasized organ-specific accumulation, unlocking potential for precision medicine applications. Strikingly, histopathological analysis confirmed their efficacy and biocompatibility, positioning B-CQDs and anticancer compound E1 as a next-generation theragnostic tool. This study paves the way for safer, more efficient nanocarriers in cancer therapy, merging cutting-edge nanotechnology with precision oncology.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quantum dots (QDs) have revolutionized biomedical research yet concerns over their metal-based toxicity have fueled the quest for safer alternatives. This study presents boron-doped carbon quantum dots (B-CQDs) as a groundbreaking nanoplatform for targeted drug delivery and bioimaging, as being biocompatible materials. Using a rapid microwave-assisted synthesis, CQDs successfully developed with exceptional fluorescence, high quantum yield, and remarkable stability. When conjugated with the anticancer agent E1, they demonstrated selective cytotoxicity against breast and lung cancer cells, sparing healthy tissues. In vivo biodistribution studies in BALB/c mice revealed using detailed virtual image analysis and visualization that administration routes (IV, IP, PO) emphasized organ-specific accumulation, unlocking potential for precision medicine applications. Strikingly, histopathological analysis confirmed their efficacy and biocompatibility, positioning B-CQDs and anticancer compound E1 as a next-generation theragnostic tool. This study paves the way for safer, more efficient nanocarriers in cancer therapy, merging cutting-edge nanotechnology with precision oncology.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the discovery of quantum dots (QDs), they are composed of inorganic substances such as cadmium and selenium, which have been produced. Their use in many areas, including health, has begun to be investigated (Probst et al., 2013). However, it has been found that the nuclei of QDs containing metal atoms such as cadmium, selenium, and gold or other inorganic substances accumulate in various compartments of the body, causing toxic effects (Soo Choi et al., 2007; Singh et al., 2018). The production of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) came after 2004, and dimensions below 10 nm. (Namdari et al., 2017). QDs with organic cores were prepared in later studies, and their use in health fields was started to be investigated (Namdari et al., 2017; Camlik et al., 2024). Carbon and graphene QDs have peerless properties, such as excellent chemical stability, selectivity, limited toxicity, good biocompatibility, and good luminescence, making them widely used in photocatalysts, sensors, bioimaging, biolabeling, and drug delivery (Lin et al., 2014; Camlik et al., 2024). A carbon source is simply needed to synthesize CQDs. Chemical substances from various biological sources such as citric acid, sodium citrate, p-phenylenediamine, or organic starting materials such as watermelon peel, mango, pineapple, and milk were reported to be selected as carbon sources (TS and RSD, 2016). CQDs were successfully synthesized from milk in the study carried out by Shabbir et al. in 2022. They were synthesized by hydrothermal synthesis using a stainless-steel autoclave (Shabbir et al., 2022). In another study, CQDs were synthesized using watermelon juice and visualized at the inside of the cells (Rawat et al., 2019). Synthesis of CQD was performed by Kostromin et al. using the citric acid by pyrolysis method (Kostromin et al., 2023).

In a study managed by Wang et al., p-phenylenediamine was selected as a starting material and CQDs were synthesized using the solvothermal method. Phenylenediamine was preferred because it showed a stable fluorescence (Wang et al., 2023). Elements which are close to carbon atoms in the periodic table, can be added to CQDs, e.g., boron (B) or nitrogen (N) (Largitte et al., 2021). In another study, it was stated that it is difficult to obtain a uniform size in the synthesized CQDs when the other atom is doped, and this would prevent further applications, especially for in vivo applications (Kou et al., 2020). Boron atoms are reported to be doped into the structure. Since then, boron-doped carbon quantum dots (B-CQDs) have attracted attention in biomedical and environmental applications due to their superior physicochemical properties (Ozkasapoglu et al., 2023). Boron imparts unique features to materials, such as heat resistance and mechanical durability. It has been found that the addition of boron atoms improves physical and chemical stability as well as the fluorescence properties of QDs. If it is the case, the electron deficiency of boron modifies the electronic structure of CQDs, resulting in higher quantum yield, enhanced luminescence, and improved sensor performance. Furthermore, the surface functionalization capacity of boron atoms enhances the biocompatibility and targetability of these nanoparticles (Zhang et al., 2024). B-CQDs are particularly utilized in the healthcare field to improve the durability of CQDs and to facilitate the delivery of active pharmaceutical and imaging agents to target tissues (Hassan et al., 2024). In summary, B-CQDs offer tunable band gaps, enhanced fluorescence, high quantum yield, customizable surface functionalities, improved biocompatibility, and activity. In addition, since the surface chemistry of CQDs obtained from different starting materials is different, the luminescence intensity and functional groups at the surfaces are different. B-CQDs represent an emerging class of nanomaterials with enhanced physicochemical and biomedical properties compared to undoped ones. The incorporation of boron into the carbon lattice imparts several distinct advantages besides mentioned ones namely: increased electron-deficient character of boron, facilitating interactions with biomolecules and enhancing cellular uptake; and a potential for synergistic anticancer activity due to boron’s ability to interfere with tumor cell metabolism and contribute to oxidative stress as well as augmented penetration ability (Das and Mohapatra, 2017). Research has shown by Largitte et al., CQDs doped with boron were prepared using different formulations It was found that the luminescence and the surface of the synthesized B-CQDs were different because their functional groups were different (Largitte et al., 2021). Therefore, QDs with different functional groups and surface properties may be targeted to different organs or tissues. Pharmacokinetic studies with nanomaterials show that they are absorbed through, especially by lymphatic vessels and macrophages (Utembe et al., 2020). It was reported that some of the synthesized metal-containing QDs passed through the cell, mostly by endocytosis and passive diffusion (Derfus et al., 2004). It is stated that the uptake of QDs into the cell varies depending on their size, interactions with the serum proteins, their density, shape, and functional groups on their surfaces (Utembe et al., 2020; Fischer et al., 2006). The distribution step following the absorption is defined as the spread of molecules to the tissues, organs, and the entire organism. Small nanoparticles passing into the blood adsorb some proteins in the blood onto their surfaces (Wang et al., 2016).

Thus, they ensure the distribution of molecules to various organs and tissues. The vascular endothelium acts as a semipermeable barrier, separating the vascular area from the extravascular area. The space between the endothelium is defined as approximately 5 nm. Nanoparticles with dimensions below 5 nm can easily pass through the endothelium. Particles larger than 5 nm may have a long circulation time in the endothelium (Utembe et al., 2020; Uhl et al., 2017). Nanoparticles can be reduced from the body through the milk, lungs, and sweat glands, and also urine and feces (Zhang et al., 2014). Molecules with a hydrodynamic diameter smaller than 5.5 nm are reported to be excreted via the kidneys after renal filtration (Soo Choi et al., 2007). In a study with silver (Ag) QDs of different sizes, it was found that QDs of 5 nm size had the maximum biological half-life and minimum clearance in animals, respectively. (Javidi et al., 2019). All these show the biodistribution of nanoparticles is rely on the dimension and surface properties.

Herein, we examine the distribution of CQDs and B-CQDs with or without an anticancer molecule (E1) when applied in various routes in mice. Their anticancer activities were determined in cell culture studies. The compound 3-(3-Benzoyl-1-phenyl-1H-pyrazol-5-carbonyl)–2H-chromen-2-one (E1) primarily exerts its antitumor effect through the inhibition of Heat Shock Proteins (HSPs), which are critically involved in the regulation of cell division. Experimental studies demonstrated that E1 exhibits potent antitumor activity against MCF-7 and Saos-2 cell lines. Molecular docking analyses indicated that E1 shows a high binding affinity toward the ATP-binding site of HSP90, thereby blocking its function and contributing to its anticancer efficacy (Koca et al., 2016). E1 substance, which was approved as an effective and potential breast anticancer drug earlier (Koca et al., 2016), was tested and found to be more effective on cancerous cells than on healthy ones in this study.

Citric acid, polyethylene glycol 3350, hydroquinone, ethylenediamine were obtained from Sigma Aldrich (Sigma Aldrich, Germany). L-cysteine was obtained from Biobasic (Biobasic, Canada). Boric acid was supplied by Tekkim (Tekkim, Turkey). p-phenylenediamine 2 % Norteks (Norteks, Turkey), 12000 Da Dialysis membrane and cellophane membrane, 100–500 Da membrane, silica gel (0.063–0.200 mm) was purchased from Merck, Germany. Production of CQDs was carried out by using a microwave reactor (Microwave 300, Anton Paar, United Kingdom). Anton Paar LiteSizer 500 (Anton Paar, England) instrument was used for characterization and stability. Spectrofluorimeter (Shimadzu RF6000, Japan) was used to determine the optical properties of CQDs. In vivo studies were performed in BALB/c male mice. In vivo studies were conducted with the permission of the Local Ethics Committee for Experimental Animals of Bezmialem University (Decision no: 2024-40-1).

Formulation F1 was prepared as follows: 0.5 g of citric acid was dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water. Then, 0.5 g of boric acid and 0.5 g of urea were added to the solution, and then the synthesis was carried out in a microwave reactor at 140 degrees for 20 min.; F2, 0.2 g of boric acid, 0.2 g of citric acid, 0.005 g of L-cysteine were dissolved in 4 mL of distilled water and for formulation F2 the reaction was same. 0.625 mL of 2 % p-phenylenediamine was dissolved in 1:1 EtOH/distilled water for formulation F3., 1 g of hydroquinone was dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water and then 0.34 mL of ethylenediamine was added, and formulation F4. Formulations F3 and F4 were produced under the same synthesis conditions applied in formulation F1 and F2.

The QDs were purified by column chromatography. In many sources in the literature, QDs are reported to be purified through a dialysis membrane, but with this method, we faced a high material loss. The column chromatography minimizes the material loss and makes the purification more suitable (Han et al., 2017). Silica gel was selected as the stationary phase and 1:1 (EtOH/Ethyl acetate) was chosen as the mobile phase. Then it was then evaporated using an evaporator (Wiggens Strike 380/ Germany) at 50 °C, 55 rpm.

QDs have unique photoluminescence properties. CQDs can be stabilized to increase their photoluminescence properties, stability and biocompatibility. For PEGylation, 0.02 g of PEG3350 was added to the formulation at 25 ± 2°C (Camlik et al., 2022).
pH measurements of CQDs were carried out with Mettler Toledo S220-K device at 25 ± 2°C. Measurements were made immediately after the synthesis and after the purifications in 3 replicates.

The particle size and zeta potential measurements of the formulations were made 6 replicates at 25 ± 2°C after being diluted 10 times. Due to the density and photoluminescence properties of CQDs, measurements in the undiluted state give erroneous results.

QY can be measured by comparing CQDs with a standard fluorescent material such as quinine sulphate solution. CQDs were dispersed in distilled water, quinine sulphate in 0.1 M sulfuric acid. The fluorescence’s were determined using the Shimadzu Spectrofluorometer RF-6000. QY was calculated using the following equation (Alkian et al., 2022)

QY represents the yield of CQDs, QYc represents the QY of the reference quinine sulfate (0.54), I symbolize the fluorescence area, A symbolizes the absorbance intensity at 360 nm excitation wavelength, η symbolizes the refractive index, c represents CQDs, and s represents quinine sulfate.
FT-IR spectroscopy was performed in the range of 4000–650 cm−1 (Probst et al., 2013) using a Shimadzu QATR-S (Shimadzu, Japan) instrument.

Membrane permeation studies were performed using two different membranes, the dialysis membrane, and the cellophane membrane. 1 ml of CQDs was added to the donor region of the Franz cell and PBS solution was added to the receptor. The temperature was set at 37 °C. Samples were taken at the 5th, 15th, 30th, 45th, 60th, 90th, 180th, 240th, and 300th minutes and analyzed by the UV–VIS spectrophotometer. The method was validated. The experiments were carried out in 3 replicates.

The stability tests were performed and at the 3rd, 6th, 9th, and 12th months particle size and zeta potential measurements were executed with the Litesizer 500 (Anton Paar/England) device at 25 ± 2°C with 6 repetitions.
CQDs dots were exposed to UV light for 6 h. Samples were analyzed by spectrofluorometer (RF6000/ Shimadzu/ Japan) at 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6 h.

Fluorescence cell imaging fixation protocol was applied. An adherent cell line was grown with a normal growth medium. When 90 % confluent, cells were lifted with trypsin and counted. 8x104 cells per plate were suspended with 400–500 µl medium and seeded in 12-well plates. Cells were placed in a 37 °C humidified 5 % CO2 cell incubator. When cells were ∼ 70 % confluent, the medium was aspirated. 12-well plates were gently washed with 1X PBS buffer, and the buffer was removed. CQDs diluted with medium in the specified concentration range were applied to the cells. Cells were placed in a 37 °C humidified 5 % CO2 cell incubator and incubated for 2–3 h. At the end of the period, each well was gently washed with 1x PBS, the washing solution was removed, and the cells were fixed with 4 % Formaldehyde (prepared with 1x PBS) for 15 min. The fixing agent was removed from the medium and the cells were carefully washed with 1xPBS.the cells were finally framed.

In our study, human ER + breast cancer MCF-7 cell line, healthy breast epithelial cell h-TERT cell line, and lung cancer A549 cell lines obtained from ATCC were used. Culture conditions were DMEM High Glucose and RPMI media containing 10 % fetal bovine serum and 1 % penicillin–streptomycin, grown at 37 °C, 5 % CO2, and 95 % humidity. Cells were seeded on freshly coated microplates with 8 ug/cm2 collagen I (BD Biosciences), and after overnight cultivation (14 h at 37 °C, 5 % CO2), CQDs were added. Fluorophore dye cocktail containing Hoechst 33,342 and BioTracker 663 Red was added to the growth medium and incubated for 45 min (37 °C, 5 % CO2). Finally, non-confocal images of live cells were taken in Zoe. Fluorophore dye cocktail, cell-permeable nuclear dye Hoechst, and cell-permeable organelle dye MitoTracker Deep Red, which accumulates in the matrix of metabolically active mitochondria, were used. The analysis combined classical segmentation steps with object measurements using a set of morphological and structure-based (Spots, Edges, and Ridges) features using Harmony image analysis software. The Hoechst channel was used for nuclear segmentation and quantification of nuclear size and staining patterns. BioTracker 663 Red was also used to assess cell shape, as well as the distribution and number of mitochondria reported as cell roundness, as well as mitochondrial structure.

Cells were seeded in 12-well plates at 8x104 cells and allowed to adhere for 24 h. After 24 h, the cell medium was removed. Hoechst dye was diluted to 1 µg/mL in a fresh, complete cell culture medium. The cell medium was removed and replaced with a fresh medium containing Hoechst dye. Cells were incubated at room temperature or 37 °C for 5–15 min, and then images were taken.
A 200uM stock solution was prepared, and a 50ug vial of lyophilized dye was dissolved in 460uL of anhydrous DMSO or DMF. Stored at −20 °C.

When cells reached confluency, the medium was removed and a pre-warmed medium containing 100 nM BioTracker Mitochondria dye was added. For suspension cells, cells were pelleted and resuspended in a medium containing diluted BioTracker Mitochondria dye. Cells were incubated at 37 °C for 15 min or longer. Images were captured with a Zoe fluorescent cell imager (Biorad) using the two different dyes.

MCF-7 and MDA-MB-231 cells were counted and seeded in 6-well plates with the appropriate medium as 300x103 in each plate and incubated for 24 h. Then, the medium in the wells was aspirated and the IC50 value was suspended with the medium and applied to the cells. Only medium was applied to the control group. The medium was removed after 48 h. The wells were washed with 1X concentration PBS buffer. 400 μL of trypsin was added to each well and kept in the incubator for 5 min to separate the cells from the plate surface. At the end of this period, a medium containing FBS was applied to the cells, collected, and centrifuged. At the end of centrifugation, the supernatant was discarded, and the pellet was suspended with 1X binding solution and centrifuged for 5 min. Then, the supernatant was discarded and 3 μL of Annexin V and 2 μL of PI dye were added and kept in the dark for 15 min at room temperature. At the end of the period, measurements were made immediately on the device.

The cell cycle was used to identify abnormal DNA cell lines in cancer cells, to estimate the associated DNA index, and to determine the cell phase distribution in these cell lines. The cells were centrifuged at 500 g for 5 min. After centrifugation, the supernatant was removed. 1 ml of buffer solution was added to the pellet and centrifuged at 500 g for 5 min. The cell concentration was then adjusted to 1x106 cells/ml. Then, solution A (trypsin in spermine tetrahydrochloride detergent buffer) was added and incubated for 10 min at room temperature. Then, 200 μL of solution B (RNAse A and trypsin inhibitor in spermine buffer) was added. After the incubation was completed, solution C (Propidium iodide (PI) in sperm buffer) was added and kept in the dark for 10 min.

Adherent cells that were 90 % confluent were removed from the flask by trypsin application and their functioning was inhibited with a medium containing 10 % Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS). Cells were collected and centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 5 min. At the end of centrifugation, the supernatant was discarded, and the cell pellet was dissolved with the medium by gentle pipetting and seeded into 96-well plates with 7x103 cells per well. It waited for 24 h for the cells to adhere to the bottom of the plate and grow. At the end of 24 h, the medium in the wells was aspirated and serial dilutions of certain concentrations and inhibitors (Control, 31.25, 62.5, 125, 250, 500, 1000, 2000, 4000 µg/ml) were applied to the wells where the cells were. It was incubated for 24, 48, 72 h of analysis. MTT analysis was performed at the end of these hours. The absorbance values ​​read because of MTT were compared with the control group and % viability values ​​were calculated to find the dose-dependent cell viability amounts. The dose that killed 50 % of the cells (IC50) was determined using these absorbance data in the GraphPad Prism 8.0.2 program. The calculated IC50 dose was applied to the cells in subsequent analyses.

BALB/c male mice were obtained from the Bezmialem University Experimental Animals Laboratory. Mice were maintained on a 12-h light/dark cycle and food and water were ad libitum. Temperature was maintained at 18–23 °C and humidity was 40–60 %. In vivo experiments were performed in accordance with the organizational guidelines of the Bezmialem University. It was carried out with the decision and approval of the Experimental Animals and Bezmialem University Ethics Review Board numbered 2024–40-1. All experiments were performed in triplicate.

BALB-c male mice were used in the study. In vivo experiments were performed on the IVIS device. To take images of the formulations in the in vivo experiments, the IVIS (PerkinElmer, USA) device was used. The formulations were mixed with physiological serum at a ratio of (1:1) and F1 and F2 formulations were administered to mice via IV, IP, and PO routes, and F3 and F4 formulations were administered via IP and PO routes. Images were taken at 30, 90, and 120 min after the application. To calculate the dose of the CQDs, images were taken on the IVIS device of the dose given in the injector. The dose calculation was made by comparing the fluorescence intensity of the given dose with the fluorescence intensity in the images.

Biodistribution images were performed by SyGlass software inspired by the work of Lu et al (Luo et al., 2025). In total, biodistribution images were examined in 7 layers. Images were obtained with the Aura (Spectral Imaging Software/Bruker/ Germany) program as shown in Fig. 5c. Region of interest (ROI) values ​​were calculated with the Living Image Software (PerkinElmer/USA) program.

After examining the distribution of B-CQDs in organs in mice, it was planned to examine the tissues with the highest concentration histopathologically. In cases where the distribution was in the organs suches, kidney, liver, skin and lung tissues were examined.

Then kidneys and liver removed from mice were fixed in 10 % neutral formalin. They were embedded in paraffin using routine light microscopy tracking methods, and paraffin sections taken with a microtome (Thermo Microm HM 340E) at 5-µm samples were taken on slides for light microscopy.

The images of the synthesized CQDs were obtained under UV light and are shown in Fig. 2a. pH measurements were made before and after the purification of CQDs. The pH values ​​of the F1 formulation were found to be 1.77 ± 0.0058 of the pure F1 formulation 7.13 ± 0.0252 of the F2 formulation 6.81 ± 0.0153, of the pure F2 formulation 7.22 ± 0.02, of the F3 formulation 6.96 ± 0.02, of the pure F3 formulation 7.32 ± 0.0153, of the F4 formulation 5.77 ± 0.0115 and the pure F4 formulation 7.11 ± 0.0115, respectively. Moreover, the hydrodynamic diameters and zeta potentials of the CQDs were measured. The hydrodynamic radii of formulations F1, F2, F3, and F4 were measured and found to be 10.42 ± 0.3913 nm, 10.76 ± 0.1626 nm, 10.91 ± 0.5325 nm, and 9.92 ± 0.4406 nm, respectively. When the zeta potential values were measured, they were found to be −18.11 ± 1.6435 mV for formulation F1, −9.1 ± 1.6740 mV for formulation F2, −8.15 ± 1.4706 mV for formulation F3, and −13 ± 3.6736 mV for formulation F4. TEM and SPM images of formulations F1, F2, F3, and F4 were obtained as seen in Fig. 2b. The QY of formulation F1 was found to be 94.017 %, the QY of formulation F2 was found to be 94.302 %, the QY of formulation F3 was found to be 88.641 % and the QY of formulation F4 was found to be 85.324 %.

FTIR experiments were performed, and related spectra are shown in Fig. 1
. The PEGylation was confirmed by comparing spectra.

It was found that the membrane permeation of F1, F2, F3, and F4 formulations differed depending on time. F1 formulation passed through the cellophane membrane at the end of the 6th hour, while the dialysis membrane passed at a rate of 49 %. F2 formulation passed through the dialysis membrane 87 %, while the cellophane membrane passed 86 % rate. F3 formulation passed through the cellophane membrane 30 %, while the dialysis membrane was 12 %. F4 formulation passed through the cellophane membrane 34 %, while the dialysis membrane was 30 %. Formulations F1, F2, F3 and F4 penetrate the dialysis membrane at 13.66 %, 44.23 %, 12.61 %, and 34.36 %, respectively. The penetration values ​​of the formulations through the cellophane membrane are 24.29 %, 42.85 %, 11.30 %, and 30.55 %, respectively. Fig. 3 c shows the synthesized CQDs' in vitro release and penetration graphs.

The changes in F1 after 12 months at 5 ± 3 ◦C, 25 ± 2◦C/60 ± 5 % RH and 40 ± 2 ◦C/75 ± 5 % RH, −20 ± 5 was 10.77 ± 0.624 nm, 10.05 ± 0.624 nm, 10.21 ± 0.062 nm, 10.21 ± 0.586 nm, respectively. The values for F2 were 10.12 ± 0.11 nm, 8.87 ± 0.05 nm, 9.91 ± 0.102 nm, 10.34 ± 0.347 nm, respectively., F3 hydrodynamic diameters were 10.9 ± 1.39 nm, 9.97 ± 0.807 nm, 9.51 ± 1.042 nm, respectively. For F4, the values were found as 9.8 ± 0.689 nm, 9.25 ± 0.431 nm, 11.41 ± 2.041 nm, and 9.96 ± 0.746 nm. It was revealed that there was no significant change in the diameter. When the photostability of the formulations was examined, it was found that the F1 formulation preserved 23 % of the emission values, the F2 formulation 60 %, the F3 formulation 57 %, and the F4 formulation 61 %. Photostability spectra are shown in Fig. 3a.

The synthesized CQDs and CQDs incubated with E1 were applied to A549, h-TERT, and MCF-7 cell lines. F1 and F1-E1 formulations were not observed in the cells. F2 and F3 were applied to the A549 cell line, the F4 showed yellow fluorescence and F4-E1 were found to be fluorescence in the cells. Of the F4-E1 h-TERT cell line, F2 showed fluorescence of both nuclear and mitochondrial areas, while F4 did not show only nuclear fluorescence, F4-E1 gave fluorescence in both nuclear and mitochondrial. F3 did not show fluorescence. Images of the h-TERT cell line are given. While F2 applied to the MCF-7 lines showed nuclear and mitochondrial luminescence, F3 and F4 were found to show only nuclear luminescence but exhibited F3-E1 nuclear and mitochondrial luminescence in the MCF-7 cell lines.

The apoptosis experiment was carried out by adding an E1 substance to the F2 formulation. The comparison was made by applying E1 substance with PEGylated CQDs, evacuated QDs, and bare CQDs to A549, h-TERT, and MCF–7 cell lines. As shown in Fig. 4, 98.21 % cell viability was obtained in F2 in the MCF-7 cell line, while 96.81 % cell viability was obtained with F2-E1. 1.82 % late apoptosis and 0.90 % early apoptosis were observed with F2-E1. 0.21 % late apoptosis and 1.33 % late apoptosis were observed in the CQDs here. It was found that CQDs preserved cell viability by 96.71 %, while F2-E1 preserved cell viability by 96.61 % in the h-TERT cell line. With F2-E1, 1.62 % late apoptosis and 0.90 % early apoptosis were observed. In the A549 cell line, F2 maintained 97.71 % cell viability, while F2-E1 maintained 96.18 % cell viability. F2-E1 showed 3.42 % late apoptosis and 0.39 % early apoptosis.

F1, F2, F3, and F4 formulations were applied to MCF-7, A549, and h-TERT cell lines at a concentration of 31.25, 62.5, 125, 250, 500, 1000, 2000 and 4000 μg/ml for cell viability studies. At a concentration of 31.25 μg/ml, F1 preserved 89.32 %, F2 86.15 %, F3 92.06 %, and F4 95.38 % cell viability. Fig. 3b shows the cytotoxicity results of the synthesized CQDs.

28–30 g BALB-c healthy male mice were used. To visualize CQDs in experimental animals, an IVIS device, which is an imaging system that allows the real-time monitoring of the distribution and activity of cells, genes, or proteins in living organisms using fluorescence markers, was used. When the F1 formulation was administered by the IP route, it showed the highest fluorescence intensity at 30 min. F1 was distributed in the spinal cord, liver, lymph, kidney, intestine, and heart, respectively. At the end of the 120th minute, it was found that the fluorescence intensity seen in the spinal cord shifted to the heart and abdomen. When the F2 formulation was administered by the IP route, it was found to accumulate in the intestine, spinal cord, lymph, testis, liver, kidney, and heart regions, respectively. It was found that the F3 formulation showed fluorescence in the liver, kidney, lung, and lymph, respectively. It was found that the F4 formulation accumulated in the intestine, testis, lymph, kidney, and heart. The IP biodistribution of CQDs is shown in Fig. 5. When the F1 formulation was administered IV, it was found that it accumulated especially in the lymph and then in tissues such as the kidney, liver, and heart. It was found that the F2 formulation showed intense fluorescence, especially in the intestine. The IV biodistribution of CQDs is shown in Fig. 6
. F1 formulation administered via the PO route showed the highest fluorescence intensity in the liver, lymph, heart, and kidney, F2 formulation in the liver, abdomen, and lungs, F3 formulation in the abdomen and heart, and F4 formulation in the abdomen. The PO biodistribution of CQDs is shown in Fig. 7 .

It was determined that F1, F2, F3, and F4 formulations did not cause any damage to brain tissues. When kidney tissue sections were examined, it was seen that F1 formulation did not cause any change when administered orally, normal glomerulus and tubule structure when administered IP, and increased glomerular mesangial matrix when administered IV. When F2 formulation was administered orally and IP, normal glomerulus and tubule structure were observed, when administered IV, dense glomerular mesangial matrix and increased pigmentation were observed. No change was observed when the F3 formulation was administered IP and PO. No damage was observed in kidney tissue when F4 was administered IP and PO. Histopathology images obtained from IP applications of the formulations are shown in Fig. 4. When liver tissue was examined, normal liver lobule structure was observed after IP and oral applications of F1 formulation. Increased vacuole appearance was observed in hepatocytes when administered IV. Increased vacuole appearance was observed when F2 formulation was administered IV. Fig. 4c shows histopathology images of formulations after IV administration. Normal liver appearance was observed when F2, F3, and F4 formulations were administered by IP and oral routes. Lymph tissue images were observed as normal in all administration routes. Testicular tissue was observed as normal in all administration routes of F1 formulation. No abnormality was detected in testicular tissue when F2 formulation was administered by IV, IP, and PO routes. When heart tissue sections were examined, normal heart muscle appearance was observed in the F2 formulation administered by the IV route. Sections of F2 formulation administered by IP and oral routes were observed as normal. All sections examined in all administration routes of F1 formulation were found to have a normal appearance. F1 and F2 formulations were observed as normal in all administration routes. No abnormality was detected in lung, testicular, and heart tissues in all administration routes of F3 and F4 formulations. When skin sections were examined, it was observed that F1, F2, F3, and F4 formulations were normal in all administration routes. Fig. 7c shows histopathology images of the formulations after oral administration.

QDs can be synthesized by many methods. But mainly divided into two: top-down and bottom-up. While the bottom-up method is based on the principle of bringing substances from molecular size to QDs crystal; the top-down method is to obtain CQDs by breaking bulk matter into small pieces. The most common bottom-up method among QDs production methods is the hydrothermal method (Yadav et al., 2023). Boron-doped QDs were synthesized using the microwave reactor synthesis method successfully. Microwave, discovered by R. Bunsen towards the end of the 18th century, is currently used in the production of QDs, which constitute almost the smallest of nanomaterials. The reaction principle with microwaves is based on heating molecules or ions by increasing the movements of the waves sent by the reactor by means of piezoelectric crystals. The most important advantages of microwave reactors are that they distribute the heat evenly and prevent heat loss, allowing a faster synthesis (Jamkhande et al., 2019). It also reduces synthesis time from hours to minutes compared to other methods (Singh et al., 2018). CQDs were produced successfully by microwave synthesis. Formulations F1 and F2 were synthesized from different starting materials. In addition, formulations F3 and F4 showing yellow and red fluorescence were also synthesized. CQDs can be doped with heteroatoms to improve their optical, physical, and physicochemical properties. CQD's fluorescence properties are known to increase when CQDs are doped with boron (B) atoms (Hassan et al., 2024). The formulation was adopted using Bourlinos et al method. It was used for the synthesis of the F1 formulation. In their study, B-CQDs were synthesized by the microwave method using a local microwave device. It was observed that the solution obtained after the synthesis was like olive green (Bourlinos et al., 2015). The F1 formulation developed in our study was synthesized in a microwave reactor. Therefore, the synthesis conditions were controlled better and the solution obtained after the reaction was observed to be crystal clear and transparent. The F2 formulation was synthesized based on the CQDs synthesized by Çamlık et al. (Camlik et al., 2022). CQDs were doped with B atoms. In the study conducted by Hassan et al, B-CQDs were also synthesized by microwave. The QY of the synthesized QDs was reported to be 31.15 % and the particle size was 3.2 nm (Hassan et al., 2024). From research conducted by Zhang et al, B-CQDs were synthesized by hydropyrolysis. The mean particle size of the produced B-CQDs was reported as 2.3 nm and the QY was 30.85 %. The synthesized F1 and F2 formulations were produced by the microwave reactor in our study. Particle sizes were found below 10 nm which is compatible with the results in literature. F2 formulation was prepared based on the CQDs formulation developed by Çamlık et al. which does not have B. It was noticed that the B addition has a positive contribution to the QB and fluorescence.

Within the scope of characterization studies of CQDs, pH measurements, particle size, and zeta potential, TEM and SPM images of QDs and in vitro penetration studies were performed. The pH value of the F1 formulation after synthesis was found to be quite low. This is thought to be due to the use of citric acid monohydrate as the starting material (Camlik et al., 2022). The physiological pH range is around 7–8 (Khan et al., 2023). The measured pH values of purified CQDs were found to be higher near pH 7 which may be more compatible with physiological pH.

Determining the particle size and zeta potential of CQDs supplies significant details about the properties of CQDs, namely the dimensions and surface properties. The particle sizes of CQDs were between 1 and 10 nm in the literature (Rani et al., 2020). The particle size of prepared CQDs measured by the dynamic light scattering (DLS), was found to be between 9 and 10 nm, indicating that the CQDs were successfully synthesized. In research, the DLS particle size of the CQDs was found to be between 6 and 7 nm (Zaini et al., 2024), this result confirms our findings. In the study conducted by Sarkar et al., the particle size of CQDs was found to be between 2 and 5 nm (Sarkar et al., 2016). To determine the exact particle sizes of CQDs, microscopic imaging such as TEM is needed but it is still not capable enough to give the average values and the distribution. However, in the TEM images we obtained, it was determined that the synthesized CQDs were smaller than 10 nm. In addition, SPM analysis was performed to show the surface topography of CQDs.

For a substance that affects biological systems, must be water-soluble to some extent. To increase the aqueous affinity of a substance the functional groups at the surface can be changed. Polyethylene glycol (PEG) is a substance used for biological and medical purposes (Peng et al., 2020). Coating the surface with PEG, in other words, PEGylation was first done in the 1960s. PEGylation was used to increase the half-life and stability of the drug and to reduce binding with serum albumin. PEGylation is also done to increase the bioavailability and stability (Shi et al., 2021). The PEGylation process may cause a slight increase in particle size (Yadav and Dewangan, 2021; Ilbasmis-Tamer et al., 2016). The particle sizes of the CQDs synthesized after PEGylation were found to be between 10 nm and 12 nm. The increased results we obtained may be because of PEGylation. This situation is thought to be an indication of successful QD coating. Based on the comparative analysis of the FT-IR spectra, significant spectral differences were observed between the original samples (F1, F2, F3 and F4) and their PEG-modified counterparts (F1-PEG, F2-PEG, F3-PEG, and F4-PEG) (Fig. 1). All spectra were recorded in the 4000–650 cm−1 range and presented in %Transmittance mode. In the spectra of F1-PEG and F2-PEG, a broad OH/NH stretching band around 3200–3600 cm−1 indicates the presence of hydroxyl groups from PEG, confirming its incorporation into the structure. Additionally, an increase in intensity in the 2800–3000 cm−1 region suggests the contribution of aliphatic C–H stretching vibrations from PEG’s long chains (Fig. 1b). Possible shifts in carbonyl bands around 1700–1750 cm−1 support the formation of new chemical bonds following PEG modification. A notable increase in the intensity of the C–O–C stretching bands in the 1050–1150 cm−1 region further confirms successful PEG integration. In the F3 sample, characteristic peaks associated with aromatic or conjugated systems were observed around 1600–1500 cm−1, while the F3-PEG spectrum exhibited additional or shifted bands, particularly a distinct C–O–C stretch around 1100 cm−1 and a broad OH band near 3400 cm−1—both indicative of PEG presence (Fig. 1f). These findings collectively demonstrate that PEG modification was successfully achieved across all samples, as supported by the FT-IR data. In the FTIR spectrum, a comparison between the F4 and F4-PEG samples indicates that PEG modification was successfully achieved. A distinct peak observed around 1100 cm−1 in the F4-PEG sample corresponds to the ether (C–O–C) bonds characteristic of PEG, while a broad peak around 3400 cm−1 signifies the presence of hydroxyl (–OH) groups from PEG. Additionally, the C–H stretching vibrations observed in the 2880–2950 cm−1 range and the C=O stretching peak near 1730 cm−1 indicate the presence of aliphatic and carbonyl-containing components in the structure (Fig. 1h). These spectral differences confirm that PEG was effectively integrated into the F4 structure, resulting in significant chemical modifications.

Electrokinetic potential, also known as zeta potential, is the distinction among the electrical double layers formed around the particle and the particle. In nanoparticulate systems, size, charge, and surface properties affect intracellular uptake (Ashrafi Tafreshi et al., 2020). Positively charged particles are accepted to have higher toxicity than negatively charged particles in general. This is due to the positively charged particle's affinity for the cell membranes, which facilitates the uptake of the particles into the cells (Peng et al., 2013). The zeta potential value of the F1 formulation was found to be −18.11 ± 1.6435 mV, the F2 formulation was found to be −9.1 ± 1.6740 mV, the F3 formulation was found to be −8.15 ± 1.4706 mV and the F4 formulation was found to be −13 ± 3.6736 mV. It was observed that the zeta potential values increased significantly after the PEGylation. In the study conducted by Shu et al., it was found that PEGylation increased the zeta potential from −41 mV to −4 mV (Suh et al., 2007). In the study conducted by Pamujula et al., it was found that the zeta potential values of the PEGylated particles were more positive (Pamujula et al., 2012).

QY is a value determined by reading an unknown sample against a reference with known fluorescence intensity. A 0.1 M hydrochloride solution of quinine sulfate with a known QY of 0.54 was selected as the reference. The QY of the F1 formulation was found to be 94.32 %, the F2 formulation was found to be 94.02 %, the F3 formulation was found to be 88.64 % and the F4 formulation was found to be 85.32 %.

In vitro, release studies were used to determine the passage time of CQDs through the pores between cells before in vivo studies. In the studies conducted within the scope of the project, F1 formulation showed 20 % release from the dialysis membrane and 40 % release from the cellophane membrane. The F2 formulation had 80 % permeation through dialysis and cellophane membranes. The F3 formulation had 30 % permeation through cellophane membranes, while it had 12 % permeation through dialysis membranes. The F4 formulation had 34 % permeation through cellophane membranes, while it had 30 % permeation through dialysis membranes (Fig. 3). The permeation percentages of the F4 formulation are shown. The zeta potential value of the F1 formulation was −18 mV, and the zeta potential of the F2 formulation was measured as −9 mV. The zeta potential is very important for understanding the repulsive and attractive forces between the particles. If the force is large or small, the repulsive forces of the substances increase. It is thought that this situation reduces the permeation of the CQDs like seen for F1 formulation. The in vitro release study conducted with CQDs in the literature is only present for the formulation of CQDs loaded into exosomes conducted in 2024. In this study, CQDs were synthesized by microwave synthesis method using 0.0073 mol L-fructose, PE-PEG. CQDs were kept in the microwave device at 800 W for 5 min. The derived solution was purified by a 3500 MWCO dialysis membrane. CQDs were then loaded into exosomes and dialysis membrane passage studies were performed again. It was found that 40 % of exosomes showed 40 % release at pH 7.4 after 24 h (Tiwari et al., 2024). In another study, CQDs were synthesized using an autoclave using the hydrothermal synthesis method from citric acid. Then, transferrin was conjugated to CQDs, and doxorubicin (Dox) was loaded. In vitro drug release was examined in two different ranges at 37 °C and two pHs, 5.8 and 7.4. In the pH 7.4 environment, CQDs released only 18 % of Dox within 10 h, while approximately 47 % was rapidly released at pH 5.2. It was thought that this situation was due to the difference in the isoelectric point of transferrin and the isoelectric point of Dox (Mahani et al., 2021).

Stability studies are the determination of the most suitable conditions for a substance to remain stable. Stability studies are very important for determining the shelf life of pharmaceutical products and creating appropriate storage conditions. Stability tests are tests performed to determine, guarantee, and prove that both the active substance and the product maintain their chemical and physical stability until the end of their shelf life. These tests are performed to determine the shelf life and usage period of an active substance or pharmaceutical product in its packaging and under storage conditions. Within the scope of our study, it was found that the synthesized CQDs were stable for 12 months under stability conditions. It was determined that the zeta potential values increased negatively in the following months. In another study, 7 different CQDs were synthesized. It was stated that the CQDs synthesized in this study maintained their stability in the long term for 3 months (Łoczechin et al., 2019). In another study, it was observed that the synthesized CQDs maintained their stability over a long period (Yang et al., 2017). As a result of photostability studies, a crucial decrease was obtained in the F1 formulation at the end of the 3rd hour, while a significant difference was observed in the other formulations, while a slower decrease was observed in the F2, F3 and F4 formulations (Fig. 3a).
Before starting cell culture studies, images of the formulations were taken under a fluorescence microscope to prove that the fluorescence image of CQDs would be taken inside the cell.
For cytotoxicity, apoptosis, and cell cycle, cell staining studies, E1 substance, which is known to be effective in resistant breast cancer and has a very low water solubility, was used.

MTT test was performed to assess the cytotoxicity that CQDs will create in cells. For the MTT test, F1, F2, F3, F4, and samples of these formulations incubated with E1 substance were applied. At a dose of 31.25 ug/ml, the F1 formulation preserved cell viability by 89.32 %; the F2 formulation by 86.15 %; the F3 formulation by 92.06 %, and the F3 formulation by 95.38 % (Fig. 3). In studies conducted with QDs, it was found that QDs preserved cell viability following the results we obtained (Jiao et al., 2018; Singh et al., 2019; Song et al., 2019) Within a study, three different metal core QDs were synthesized by continuous shell precursor injection method. Synthesis QDs were cultured in SK-BR-3 human breast cancer cell line and WI-38 normal human cell fibroblast and analyzed by MTT test at the end of 24 and 48 h. Cytotoxicity of CdSe/ZnS QDs was found to be higher than other formulations (Sukhanova et al., 2022).

Hoechst and BioTracker were used for cell staining tests. CQDs were applied to A549, h-TERT, and MCF-7 cell lines. Before application, it was checked with a fluorescence microscope to see whether the CQDs were imaged or not. F1 and F1-E1 formulations did not show any image in the applied cell lines. This may be due to the more positive zeta potential value of the F1 formulation. F2 and F2–E1. showed fluorescence in all cell lines. F2–E1 formulations gave images in all cell lines. Therefore, F2 and F2-E1 formulations were selected for apoptosis and cell cycle tests. It was determined that the F2 formulation applied to the h-TERT cell line entered both the nucleus and mitochondria. It is known that CQDs are selective for the negatively charged mitochondrial membrane (Al-Hetty et al., 2023; Dinger et al., 2024). The nuclear membrane of cells has a very tight pore structure, and nanomaterials with diameters smaller than 20 nm are reported to pass through the pores of the nucleus (Dinger et al., 2024). In another study, CQDs were used for nucleic acid detection in living cells. The results obtained showed that CQDs passed the nuclear membrane (Han et al., 2019). The results we found with the F2 formulation are consistent with the literature. It was found that the F4 formulation applied to h-TERT cells passed into the nucleus but not into mitochondria. It was found that the F4–E1 formulation passed into both the nucleus and mitochondria. When the conjugate is bound to the QDs, zeta potential values may change. It is thought that the E1 substance bound to the F4 formulation positively affects the zeta potential value. It was also found that the F3–E1 formulation showed mitochondrial luminescence. For apoptosis and cell cycle tests, unstained cells, stained cells, F2 and F2-E1 formulation-applied cell lines were compared. It was found that F2-E1 in the MCF–7 cell line caused the cells into early apoptosis compared to healthy, stained, and F2 formulation-applied cell lines and reduced the cell viability rate by 4 % compared to the F2 formulation. Similarly, it was observed that the F2-E1 formulation applied to the A549 cell line caused the cells to undergo early apoptosis relative to the F2, stained and control. No important difference was detected in the healthy breast tissue cell line hTERT. This situation shows that the F2-E1 formulation is selective for cancer cells. When the apoptosis test results were compared with the live cell imaging results, it was observed that the F2-E1 formulation caused nucleus enlargement and mitochondrial activity decrease in the cells compared to the control group. The anticancer agent referred to as “E1” in the study is chemically identified as 3-(3-benzoyl-1-phenyl-1H-pyrazol-5-carbonyl)–2H-chromen-2-one, a hybrid molecule incorporating coumarin and pyrazole pharmacophores, both known to have anticancer potential. In the experimental design, E1 was incubated with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) synthesized using boric acid, citric acid, and L-cysteine. These E1-conjugated CQDs were subsequently applied to MCF-7, A549, and hTERT cell lines for apoptosis assays. The results demonstrated that the conjugated system induced early apoptosis in MCF-7 and A549 cancer cells, while having no significant apoptotic effect on the healthy hTERT cells, indicating selective cytotoxicity. The mechanism of action and the targeting are associated with E1’s high binding affinity to the ATP-binding site of heat shock protein 90 (HSP90), a molecular chaperone that is essential for the survival and proliferation of many cancer cell types. The targeting could be because of higher cellular penetrations due to the enhanced permeability and retention effect. The unique size and the high thermodynamic activity of CQDs result in augmented penetration. Molecular docking studies suggest that E1 inhibits HSP90 function by competitively binding to this domain. In addition, the redox-sensitive nature of the CQDs, triggered by tumor microenvironment characteristics such as elevated glutathione levels, facilitates controlled drug release specifically within cancer cells. Both the chemical identity and the mechanism of action of E1 are addressed in the study through structural definition, docking studies, and biological assays, supporting its therapeutic relevance and functional efficacy when delivered via the nanocarrier system. Fig. 4 shows the apoptosis test of F2 and F2-E1 formulations. E1 is a potent anticancer agent exhibiting high selectivity toward heat shock protein (HSP) binding regions. In vitro studies have demonstrated that E1 displays significant cytotoxic activity against MCF-7 and Saos-2 cancer cell lines, as it was clearly stated in the patent application (2018-GE-119328). While many conventional chemotherapeutic agents are effective in targeting cancer cells, their lack of specificity often results in collateral damage to healthy tissues. Notably, when E1 is incorporated into a drug delivery system (BCQDs = formulation F2), it minimizes toxicity to normal cells and selectively induces early-stage apoptosis in cancer cells, highlighting its potential as a safer and more targeted therapeutic strategy. It was found to be consistent with the apoptosis test results seen in the literature. Another study, CQDs were obtained by sonication of nanodiamonds in bulk. For the apoptosis test, NP69 (normal immobilized nasopharyngeal epithelial cells), RAW264.7 (murine macrophage cells) and CNE2 (human nasopharyngeal carcinoma cells) and HepG2 (human hepatocellular carcinoma cells) cells were incubated with CQDs for 48 h. As a result, although Raw264.7 was found to be more sensitive to CQDs than other cell lines, an important difference wasn't reported between CQDs and the negative control group in other cell lines (Tian et al., 2020).

In recent years, the use and biodistribution potential of CQDs in the field of health has been started to be investigated. In the biodistribution studies of CQDs, markers such as 99mTc or the unique fluorescence properties of QDs are used as markers (Ganji Arjenaki et al., 2024; Marouzi et al., 2021). One of the most used devices for fluorescence imaging is the IVIS device in animal studies. In our project, the in vivo imaging and distribution study for F1 and F2 formulations was used, and images were framed using the IVIS device. F1 and F2 formulations were administered to experimental animals via IV, IP, and PO routes, and F3 and F4 formulations were administered through IP and PO. All measurements were taken at 0, 30, 90, and 120 min.

Starting from the 30th minute after the administration, the F1 formulation was seen especially in the lymph tissue and after that, they were found in the kidneys, lungs, heart, and abdomen. When the F1 formulation was administered IP, it was found that CQDs were accumulated in the abdomen, spinal cord, and intestine at the 30th minute. F1 formulation was found that the photoluminescence intensity decreased at the 90th and 120th minutes. When the F1 formulation was administered via the PO route, it was found that it was distributed in the lymph tissue and kidneys at the 30th minute, and after the 90 min, it was found in the spinal cord as well as the lymph and kidney tissues. Fig. 5 shows a distribution of the formulations when applied via the IP route. When the F2 formulation was given through IV routes, it was found that they accumulated in the kidney and lymph tissue, and when applied IP, it was found that it distributed in the heart, kidney, and lymph tissue, spinal cord, and when applied through PO route, they distributed in the lymph, testis, kidney, and heart tissues. The distribution of F1 and F2 formulations when administered via the IV route is shown in Fig. 6.

The results obtained showed that they were distributed to different organs and tissues from different applications. In addition, it was found that QDs synthesized with different starting materials were distributed to different parts of the body. When the F3 formulation was applied as IP, radiation intensity was observed in the testicles, kidneys, lymph tissue, and thoracic region. After the 90th minute, the intense radiation in the thoracic region decreased considerably, and only the kidneys were observed in the abdominal area. When the F3 formulation was applied as PO, it was found to be distributed in the kidneys, heart, and lymph tissue. When the F4 formulation was applied as IP, it was found to be collected in the kidneys and testicles, and when it was used as PO, it was found to be collected in the abdomen and lymph tissue. Fig. 7 shows the distributions of the formulations when administered via the PO route.

In a study performed by Geng et al., images of the CQDs after the application in mice in which a tumor model was created were taken with the IVIS device at certain time intervals. In the images acquired, it was thought that CQDs accumulated in the kidney and liver and could be excreted through the hepatobiliary system (Geng et al., 2018). Similarly, in the images obtained with the IVIS device, intense reflection was observed especially in the kidney region belonging to CQDs. In a study conducted by Su et al., CdTe QDs were administered to BALB/c female mice IV. The results showed that they were mostly distributed in the kidney and liver (Su et al., 2011). Pharmacokinetic analyses were performed from the distribution profiles obtained. According to a study, Mn/CQD/SiO2@naproxen QDs were synthesized by the solvothermal synthesis method. Images were taken at 15, 30, 45, 90, and 120 min of QDs applied to BALB/c mice. The highest plasma concentration was found at 90 min. It was found that the synthesized QDs were excreted via the liver via the hepatobiliary system (Ardestani et al., 2021). The results obtained in our study show that CQDs reached the highest concentration at 30 min. in IP administration and 5 min. in IV and PO administration. In the study conducted by Liao et al. in 2021, CQD@PEI nanoparticles with dimensions around 100 nm, synthesized by the hydrothermal method at 150 °C for 48 min, were administered IV to mice in which a tumor model was created. The fluorescence intensities of the synthesized nanoparticles were compared. It was found that the most distribution was in the liver until the 4th hour and in the tumor tissue at the 30th hour (Liao et al., 1872). According to the results we obtained, since the QDs we synthesized with a smaller size than 10 nm, they start to disperse in the body starting from the beginning but are more noticeable at the 30th minute and begin to be eliminated at the end of the 120th minute.

According to the histopathological examination results, it was found that the synthesized CQDs caused some changes in the kidney and liver tissue with IV application. When kidney tissue sections were examined, an increase in glomerular mesangial matrix and a slight increase in pigmentation were observed when the F1 formulation was applied IV. F2 formulation was applied IV, and an intense increase in glomerular mesangial matrix, an increase in filtration interval, and an intense increase in pigmentation was observed. F1 formulation was applied to IV, and an increased vacuole appearance and increased pigmentation were observed in the hepatocytes in the liver tissue. The liver tissue was examined, and normal liver tissues were observed with the IP and oral application of the F1 formulation. When the F2 formulation was applied IV, an increase in pigmentation in the liver tissues, and a normal tissue appearance was observed when it was applied IP and orally. A biodistribution study with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) synthesis by Nurunnabi et al., was carried out in 2013. As a result of histopathology tests, it was found that the given dose caused hepatotoxicity in mice but not in rats (Nurunnabi et al., 2013). In another study, the effects of GQDs produced by sonication from L-glutamic acid on the reproductive cycles of female and male mice were investigated. Lung, liver, kidney, heart, and spleen tissues of GQDs administered orally and IV were examined histopathologically. There weren’t major differences observed in the results compared to the control group. The synthesized GQDs have a charge of −30.4 ± 2.4 eV. Therefore, it was found that GQDs cannot easily pass the blood–brain barrier and will be rapidly excreted (Zhang et al., 2019). In another study, diammonium citrate (AC) based, spermidine trihydrochloride (SHC) based, and AC and SHC-based CQDs were synthesized by the sonication method. The hepatotoxicity of three different CQD formulations administered orally to BALB-c mice at a dose of 2 g/kg was evaluated. The findings showed that different starting materials had different effects on the liver, and when SHC-based CQDs were applied compared to other formulations, steatosis, and mild mixed inflammatory cell infiltration were found in the liver tissue, and when blood samples were examined, significantly increased ALT and AST levels were found. The results obtained show that the passage of CQDs into hepatocyte cells is largely dependent on their surface charges (Chen et al., 2024). When the results in the literature are compared, it was noticed that CQDs accumulated mostly in the kidney and liver, which is consistent with the findings we obtained in the studies. It is also stated that the uptake and distribution into the tissues will vary, affected by the structure of the CQD itself and the way it is administered. As the surface charges of CQDs shift towards positive value, the rates of uptake into the tissues also increase. This situation causes an increase in cellular toxicity. The passed amount of CQDs into the systemic circulation is 100 % after IV administrations, it will be possible to see morphological changes in the organs where QDs accumulate the most. When the surface charges of the QDs, we synthesized were compared to CQDs that did not show very high toxicity in IV applications same as in the literature. It was stated that if the surface charge is more positive, slight toxicity will be seen. Therefore, it was found that there may be some changes in the kidney and liver tissues because of IV application.

When lymph tissues were evaluated histopathologically, no morphological changes were observed in the tissues in all application routes. In a study on the accumulation of QDs in lymph nodes, the biodistribution of Cadmium (Cd) and Indium (In)-based QDs was monitored after subcutaneous injection. It was reported that QDs accumulated intensively in the kidney immediately after injection. It was also observed that the synthesized QDs accumulated only in regional lymph nodes. As a result of their histological study, no morphological changes were observed in the lymph tissue (Helle et al., 2012). In another study, In-based QDs were injected subcutaneously into female rats under their paws. The findings showed that localization occurred in the lymph nodes a few minutes after injection and the photoluminescence remained stable for a long time (Yaghini et al., 2016). When heart tissue sections were examined, all sections examined in all routes of administration of F1 formulation were normal. Normal heart muscle was observed in the F2 formulation administered by the IV route. Sections of F2, F3, and F4 formulations administered by IP and oral route were observed to be normal. Lung sections were found to be normal in all routes of administration of F1, F2, F3, and F4 formulations. In another study, synthesized GQDs were applied to IRC mice via intranasal and IP routes. Histological studies showed that collagen I increased in the lung tissue. As a result, although the fibrotic effects of GQDs were not severe, it was observed that high-dose exposure could cause fibrosis in the lungs, liver, and kidneys (Wu et al., 2022; Li et al., 2019). In the study conducted by Li et al., CdSe/ZnS QDs were administered to BALB/c mice via the IV route. No pathology was detected in the heart tissue in histopathology studies (Li et al., 2019). In an investigation conducted by Roberts et al., (2013) CdS-coated CdSe QDs were administered intratracheally to rats. The results showed that the QDs caused very high toxicity. In the histopathology studies we conducted within the scope of our study, we found that the CQDs given to the animals did not cause any changes in the lung tissue and the tissues appeared to be normal. In the current literature, CQDs are reported to be biodegradable and non-toxic compared to QDs with metal cores or shells such as Cd, Se, Au.

QDs, which constitute the smallest group of nanomaterials in terms of size, have obtained crucial focus in the present era due to their special fluorescence properties, very small dimensions, and quantum mechanical properties. QDs can be produced from many different starting materials and many atoms can be added to QDs. Within the scope of our study, B-CQDs and CQDs were synthesized, and characterization studies, stability studies, in vitro, in vivo, and histopathology studies were carried out. It was found that the synthesized CQDs had very high QY and were stable for 12 months under different environment. As a result of in vitro tests performed with the obtained QDs, it was determined that the selectivity of F2 formulation against cancer cells was quite high. F1 and F2 formulations were administered to BALB/c male mice via IV, IP, and PO routes; F3 and F4 formulations were administered via IP and PO routes and images were taken at certain time intervals. It was found that the F1 formulation showed the highest fluorescence in the spinal cord when applied by the IP route, in the lymph by the IV route, in the liver when applied by the PO route, the F2 formulation showed the highest fluorescence in the abdomen, F3 formulation in the lymph and liver, F4 formulation showed the highest fluorescence in the abdomen and testicles. The obtained results show that different administration routes and different starting materials play important roles in biodistribution. The target organ can be changed by changing the starting materials and co-doped atoms of CQDs. In histopathology results, it was found that the formulations did not cause tissue damage when applied by IP and oral routes. Our data revealed very important findings for the use of CQDs as drug delivery systems.

N.U.O. and I.T.D. designed the study; G.K.G. performed most of the experiments and analyzed the data. M.E.O. performed in vivo experiments and analyses. Ş.A. and G.Ç. performed histopathology experiments and analyses. M.N.A., L.G., and Y.T. performed the in vitro studies and analyses. The writing of this article was supervised by N.U.O. and I.T.D. and was carried out with the participation of G.K.G., M.E.O., Ş.A., Y.T., N.U.O., and I.T.D.
